title: Project Overview
description: Start with README.md to understand what TauGo is: the executable kernel of the τ operator (monograph chap. III.8). It lays out the doctrine and anti-goals (TauGo is a decision kernel, not a framework, orchestrator, or LLM wrapper), the single public API Kernel.Decide returning one of three regimes (Deterministe | Probabiliste | Refus), and the strict 4-layer Clean Architecture. PRD.md is the canonical V0.3 spec that the rest of this tour traces through actual code, and CLAUDE.md captures the operational rulebook including the 7 guarded anti-patterns.

title: Binary Entry Point
description: cmd/tau/main.go is where the tau binary boots. It dispatches the decide and calibrate subcommands and runs the JSON-in / JSON-out Decide flow by building a dispatcher through app.NewDispatcher. The runnable examples/quickstart shows the same path from the Go-embedding side, calling Decide on three typed cases and printing the regime plus the ventilated dimension scores. Together they answer 'how does the kernel get invoked?' before we look at what it does.

title: The Public API: Kernel.Decide
description: internal/tau/operator.go is the sole public entry point of the τ core. It declares the Kernel.Decide interface and the canonical types every other layer speaks in: Exchange (the input), Decision and Trace (the fully-instrumented output), Score, Regime, and DiscoveryMode. This is the contract the README promised — one function, three possible regimes. Any change here risks an API rupture, which is why it is flagged as a sensitive module.
languageLesson: In Go, the public surface is an interface plus exported types in a single package. Capitalized identifiers (Kernel, Exchange, Decide) are exported; lowercase ones stay package-private. Keeping the whole contract in one file makes the 'API rupture' boundary explicit and reviewable.

title: The Validity Frontier
description: Building on the Exchange type from Step 3, internal/tau/frontier.go implements FrontierCheck — the first-rank guard that gates whether the kernel may even proceed. It encodes the four classic frontier conditions; Inside() returns true only when none of them are met, otherwise the exchange is out-of-frontier and the kernel must Refus. Bypassing this guard is forbidden anti-pattern #2. diagnostics.go declares the canonical Refus diagnostic constants (DiagFrontiereFranchie, DiagVerrouOntologique, ...) that the frontier and later steps reference.

title: The Three Dimensions
description: Once past the frontier, τ scores an Exchange along three orthogonal dimensions, each in [0,1]. D-SENS (dsens.go) measures where meaning is fixed, weighting four probes including a reasoner-intent probe that consults the injected LLM client. D-AUTORITÉ (dauthority.go) measures the reach of the delegation chain. D-INVARIANT (dinvariant.go) measures support for integration invariants. score.go aliases the shared Score type and the clamp01 helper that bounds every probe to [0,1].
languageLesson: The score.go alias (type Score = tau.Score) exists to break an import cycle: dimensions must speak the core's Score type without importing back into a package that imports dimensions. Go forbids import cycles outright, so type aliases are an idiomatic way to share a type across a layered boundary (ADR-0008).

title: The Five Invariants I1-I5
description: The three dimensions feed the five refutable invariants of chap. III.8.5. evaluator.go aggregates them into a single Statuses struct and exposes EvaluateInvariants, which runs I1 (conservation) through I5 (conjunctive composition) over an Exchange/Decision pair. i3_authority_asymmetry.go is the most consequential: it encodes Searle's 1995 institutional-fact asymmetry, forcing an ontological Refus when authority is blocked without an institutional attestation — or when the calibration profile has expired (a theme picked up in Step 9).

title: Refuting Invariants by Fuzzing
description: The invariants are not asserted — they are continuously attacked. fuzz_targets_test.go defines native Go fuzz targets FuzzI1..FuzzI5 that throw randomized Exchanges at each invariant to try to refute its property (chap. III.8.7). This is how TauGo treats its theory as falsifiable: an invariant survives only as long as no counterexample is found. This step shows the empirical backbone the README's invariant-status table rests on.
languageLesson: Go's built-in fuzzing (func FuzzXxx(f *testing.F)) is a first-class part of the standard testing toolchain since Go 1.18. The engine mutates a seed corpus and re-runs the property function millions of times, persisting any crashing input as a regression seed — turning an invariant into an executable, refutable claim.

title: The 8-Step Dispatcher
description: internal/orchestration/dispatcher.go is where everything composes: it implements Kernel.Decide as an 8-step pipeline whose ordering is deliberate (PRD §10). The steps run the frontier check (Step 4), the D-AUTORITÉ ontological lock, the profile-expiry guard, ventilated D-SENS/D-INVARIANT scoring (Step 5), weighted regime classification, and the I1-I5 invariant evaluation (Step 6) — always returning a fully traced Decision. This is the orchestration layer the Clean Architecture keeps strictly below the tau core.

title: Calibration & Drift
description: The dispatcher's thresholds and weights come from a calibration profile. profile.go defines the Profile and Weights structures and DefaultProfile — the checked-in default that pins thresholds, the monograph version, and a date_revision. drift.go guards that profile: it flags it as stale when CPU, LLM, or corpus fingerprints no longer match the environment, or when the date_revision has passed (anti-pattern #3, the I3 expiry path from Step 6). A stale profile is not silently tolerated; it forces a Refus.

title: The Bridges: LLM & AgentMeshKafka
description: TauGo never embeds a concrete LLM. client.go declares a narrow Client interface (Fingerprint + Interpret) that the D-SENS probe from Step 5 consumes, and stub.go provides a deterministic Stub used by default so tests stay reproducible. The AgentMeshKafka bridge (adapter.go defines neutral DTOs and the streaming interface; file_adapter.go streams JSONL fixtures as those DTOs) connects the kernel to empirical traffic without leaking infrastructure types into the core. This is how the architecture isolates non-determinism at the edges.
languageLesson: Dependency inversion in Go: the core declares a small consumer-side interface (Client) and accepts any implementation. The concrete LLM is injected at the app layer, while a deterministic Stub satisfies the same interface in tests — keeping the tau core free of any vendor import (anti-pattern #6).

title: Wiring It Together
description: internal/app/app.go is the application lifecycle layer that the entry point from Step 2 actually calls. NewDispatcher wires the DefaultProfile from Step 9 and the selected LLM backend from Step 10 (the deterministic stub unless real is configured) into an orchestration dispatcher from Step 8. This is the one place where concrete dependencies are injected, satisfying the Clean Architecture rule that the inner tau core never reaches outward.

title: Architecture Enforcement
description: The whole tour's layering is not a convention you have to trust — it is mechanically enforced. arch_test.go walks the package AST to keep the Clean Architecture sealing rules intact: tau/* may not import orchestration or bridge, and concrete LLM imports are banned outside app/ and bridge/llm/. anti_patterns_test.go guards the doctrine itself: no exported predictive API, the I3 date_revision is respected, and unmodeled observations are always reported. These tests are the capstone that keeps every boundary you just learned from eroding.
languageLesson: These are Go tests that import go/ast and go/parser to inspect the source tree itself rather than its runtime behavior — 'architecture tests'. A forbidden import makes the test suite fail like any other red test, turning Clean Architecture rules into CI-grade guarantees (here, local make test).
